beats.text: A model knows only what it was trained on. Retrieval feeds it the relevant passages at query time instead.

visual.sub: Grounding a frozen model in external facts

beats.text: Parametric memory has four chronic problems: freshness, private knowledge, grounding, and context economy.
Weights freeze at the training cutoff, so anything that changed afterward is invisible until you retrain.
Pasting the whole knowledge base into every prompt is quadratic in attention cost and hits the window limit.
Most wrong answers are recall bugs: the right passage was never retrieved, not a reasoning failure.

visual.label: Reasoning fixes for a missed passage

beats.text: RAG has two phases: indexing runs offline once per corpus, and retrieval runs online per request.
Indexing splits documents into chunks, embeds each chunk into a vector, and loads them into a searchable index.
At query time the question is embedded, the index returns nearest chunks, a reranker reorders, and survivors are assembled.
Query and documents must share the same vector space, or measured nearness between them is meaningless.
End-to-end answer quality is bounded above by retrieval quality, so we spend more pages on retrieval.

concepts.title: Chunking: the unglamorous decision that dominates

beats.text: An embedding represents a whole chunk as one vector, so the chunk is the atomic unit of retrieval.
Small chunks are semantically focused and score high on precision, but often lack the surrounding context.
Large chunks carry full context and help recall, but one vector averages many topics and dilutes the signal.
Chunk count advances by the stride, chunk size minus overlap, not by the chunk size itself.
A 6000-token document with chunk size 500 and overlap 50 tiles into 14 chunks, two more than without overlap.
Overlap is pure storage cost: doubling it roughly doubles the redundant tokens you embed and store.
An embedding model maps a chunk to a fixed-length vector so that semantically similar text lands nearby.

visual.sub: Vectors, cosine, and the vector index

beats.text: Cosine similarity compares only the direction of query and document vectors, ignoring magnitude.
Query (1,2,2) against document (2,3,1) gives a cosine of 0.8909, so they point in a similar direction.
Exact k-nearest-neighbour search scans every vector at O(Nd) cost, fine for thousands and ruinous for millions.
Production systems use HNSW or IVF indexes that answer approximate neighbours in sub-linear time.
An ANN index that silently returns the ninth-best neighbour is a recall leak you mistake for a model failure.
Dense embeddings are not the only way to rank documents, and on their own they are not the best.
BM25 scores by exact term overlap and is unbeatable at matching product codes, error strings, and proper nouns.

visual.tex: \text{BM25}(q, D) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t)\, \frac{f(t, D)\,(k_1 + 1)}{f(t, D) + k_1\left(1 - b + b\,\frac{|D|}{\overline{|D|}}\right)}

beats.text: Term-frequency saturation means the tenth occurrence adds far less than the first, so keyword stuffing cannot win.
Dense retrieval matches car won't start to engine fails to crank, with zero shared content words.
Reciprocal Rank Fusion sums a discounted reciprocal of each ranker's position, softened by a constant K near 60.
A document ranked first by BM25 and third by the dense retriever scores about 0.0323 by RRF.
The retriever surfaces a generous candidate set fast, but that set is usually mis-ordered.
The bi-encoder embeds query and document separately, so only their final vectors meet at a dot product.
A cross-encoder feeds query and document together, letting every query token attend to every document token.
The two-stage pattern narrows millions to dozens cheaply, then perfects the order of those dozens accurately.
HyDE embeds a hypothetical answer, which lives near real answers and retrieves better than the bare question.
Reranking matters most at small k: a tighter context budget lets the first few passages decide the answer.
How you arrange the reranked passages in the prompt is not cosmetic; position changes how the model uses them.
Liu and colleagues found accuracy as a function of the gold passage position is U-shaped, not flat.
Put the most relevant passages first and last, not buried in the middle where attention is weakest.
Fewer, better passages beat more, noisier ones: padding adds distractors and pushes gold toward the dead zone.
A context-only instruction that says answer only from the passages measurably reduces ungrounded answers.
RAG has two stages to evaluate, and conflating the retriever and the generator is the common measurement mistake.
Recall@k counts the relevant chunks that made the top-k; precision@k counts how many returned were relevant.
With 5 relevant chunks and 3 of them in the top-10, recall@10 is 0.6, the ceiling on what the model can use.
Faithfulness asks whether every claim in the answer is entailed by the retrieved passages.
The diagnostic table maps six failure modes from stale index to ungrounded answer onto a first fix each.

concepts.title: Agentic RAG, and the honest decision

beats.text: Naive RAG is one retrieval and one generation; it is the right default and solves most cases.
Agentic RAG makes retrieval a tool the agent calls, deciding whether to retrieve, refine, or answer.
It is strictly more capable and more expensive, inheriting agent failure modes like loops and bad tool args.
RAG, long context, and fine-tuning are complementary: tuning teaches behaviour, RAG supplies current facts.
Long context costs 0.90 per query at 80% correct; RAG costs 0.45 at 70%, so compare cost per resolved task.
Reach for RAG precisely when the knowledge is large, private, changing, or must be cited.
